cards.front: Where are C-style string literals stored in memory?
cards.back: String literals are stored in read-only memory (often called the code segment or data segment), not on the stack or heap.

cards.text: String literals should always be assigned to {{c1::const char*}}, never {{c2::char*}}, because they're in read-only memory

cards.question: What's wrong with this code?
cards.answer: Undefined behavior! Attempting to modify a string literal in read-only memory.
cards.explanation: String literals are const. Use const char* to prevent modification, or copy to a char array if modification is needed.

cards.text: The declaration {{c1::char str[] = "Hello";}} creates a modifiable copy, but {{c2::const char* str = "Hello";}} points to read-only memory

cards.front: What is a symbolic constant in the context of C-style strings?
cards.back: A symbolic constant is a named identifier representing a string literal, typically declared as const char* to prevent modification and clearly indicate the value shouldn't change.

cards.question: Will this comparison work correctly?
cards.answer: Maybe! It compares pointer addresses, not contents. Behavior depends on string literal pooling.
cards.explanation: Compilers may optimize identical string literals to the same memory location, but this isn't guaranteed. Use strcmp() for reliable string comparison.

cards.text: String literals have {{c1::static storage duration}}, meaning they exist for the {{c2::entire program lifetime}}

cards.front: Is it safe to return a pointer to a string literal from a function?
cards.back: Yes, because string literals have static storage duration and exist for the entire program lifetime. They won't be destroyed when the function returns.

cards.concept: Best practices for C-style string constants
cards.explanation:
When working with string constants:
  • Always use const char* for string literals
  • Use std::string_view (C++17) for modern const string references
  • Use std::string for modifiable strings
  • Never try to modify string literals
  • Use strcmp for comparisons, not ==

cards.question: What's the difference between these two declarations?
cards.answer: str1 points to read-only memory. str2 is a modifiable array containing a copy of "Hello".
cards.explanation: str1 is a pointer to a string literal (const). str2 is an array initialized with a copy of the string (modifiable).
